replies.text:
I love how so many commenters who weren't alive in the 80s, have been peppering social media with declarations that are just as preposterous as Will's "gay speech." They take it to the other extreme, where they claim that gay teens lived in mortal fear of being outted, which would almost certainly result in them being dragged behind the back of a pick-up truck. A good old fashioned "FxgDrxg."

This is the equivalent of claiming that cheating husbands in the 90s were in constant fear of their wife cutting off their weiner while they are asleep. Yes, it actually happened.... but it was NOT a frequent occurrence. The truth is, coming out as gay or being outted was only slightly worse than playing soccer. You'd just wear shorter shorts than most people and run with a limp wrist. Seriously tho, it's like the ridiculous bullies of this show. There has never been a real-life version of Angela & friends. 

All of those people would have gotten their asses kicked badly for acting like that. That feeling of injustice & rage that viewers feel in those scenes are not the result of social progress. It is a baser instinct. The boys from Cobra Kai would have swept Angela's leg in a heartbeat. And then put her in a bodybag. Also, Billy's dad is a hero. Billy was a punk. No kid on the planet would ever speak to their dad the way Billy did in that s3 episode. Especially not a kid living in a nice house and driving around in a sick car at 18 years old.

replies.text: ​@HarveyDarvey1122what was that island? Purgatory? Government experiment? How was it travelling in time? What was the cloud monster? Why was it coming after people? Why was the d-ead father showing up? What was the point of the whole story???

replies.author: @HarveyDarvey1122
replies.text:
​​@carolinesa911No, the island wasn't purgatory, and it wasn't anything to do with the government. It was essentially a corrupted "garden of Eden" that existed outside of our plain. It was "travelling time" because the island isn't bound to the rules of the 3rd dimension, it exists in all time and no time.
The black smoke monster is Jacob's brother, they are both bound by supernatural rules in which they are the joint protectors of the island, and therefore cannot leave. They can't kill eachother, so the entire point of the story is, Jacob was searching for somebody to replace him as the Island's protector, and he'd been bringing people to the island to vet them as candidates, as he was afraid his brother would find a way to kill him. Meanwhile his brother was ironically manipulating these candidates to plot a way to kill Jacob.
When you say "The Dead father", I assume you mean Kristian, Jack's father.
The man in black is able to take the form of people who have died on the island, or are dead on the island. Jack's Dad was in the coffin in Flight 817, he was using Kristian as a way to manipulate Jack to fulfil his plan. Eventually he found Ben was the easiest person to manipulate into killing Jacob. 

There are many points to the whole story. It's a story of Science vs Faith and how the truth is in the middle. You can take away from the story that our own individual stories are all that should really matter to us. You're not defined by the universe around you, but by your own small roles.
Kate, Sawyer etc they were all there on the island and their stories didn't matter to the eventual fate of the island, but they did matter to them all individually, as each of them went through a personal arc of redemption.

author: @Bep1
text: I figured out what’s wrong with these “coming out as gay” scenes we often see nowadays. Netflix treats it as if it is a bad thing to do, but make all the characters support it. They make it such a big deal - Will stops everyone’s preparation and drags them into a room to tell them hes gay. Like we do not care, you are about to fight a giant evil monster. If they actually wanted to convert viewers into gay allies, dont make “coming out” scenes so big. The way they did it with Robin was fine, and it fit well into the story. It even introduced a new level of relationship tension - now Steve has to find another girl. But Wills was so bad because the contrast between treating coming out like a bad thing (making it a huge deal, interrupting the preparation) and everyone being in full support of it instantly just does not work. Also, he already had his abilities before this scene, and they didn’t get better after this scene. A huge deal for something super unnecessary and unneeded for the plot.

replies.text:
​​@Dr._GroovyDude Honestly season 1 thru 4 was insanely better. They also handled him being gay insanely well in those seasons, because its obvious he was different than the other guys without it being to on the nose. Also the speech his brother gives him in season 4 telling him without telling him he knows he is gay and loves him anyway was just amazing and very on point for 1980s. Because he was out without being out because he knew it would cause issues including being beaten for it in small town americana. So he kept it to himself like a lot of gay people did back in the late 1900s. 
The story lines also were better with Steve and Dustins growing brotherhood, Lucas and Max falling in love even though her brother was racist, and so on. Even the racist brother had a complex story and redemption arch of sorts sacrificing himself for her and saying he was sorry. Then season 4 her dealing with her complex depression and emotions over loosing him. Jamie Campbell Bower was insanely good as the villian and honestly the whole show could've ended in season 4 after the gates were opened and half of the town destroyed. But the writers strike really messed with production and it took way to long to do. All the actors look like they are in their 20s and 30s not teenagers, the story isn't as complex and high stakes as other seasons. A lot of talking and introducing new characters which didn't need to be there. Only one who dies is a character people barely cared about and Elevens death is kind of a cop out at the end with saying she probably alive somewhere. It's honestly no wonder fans looked at the errors of filming and plot and thought their had to be a secret part 4 after there weird 3 part holiday drops.

author: @MoreAwsomeMetal
text:
Did someone tell the Duffer brothers that playing D&D in the 80's or 90's as well as any geek orientated hobby (role playing games, Magic the Gathering, Warhammer Fantasy Battle etc...) was seen by the girls out there as the most repulsive stuff a dude could be doing as a hobby?
I know, I played all those in the 90's, and I saw the girl's faces when they saw us playing it.

replies.author: @CarloisBuriedAlive
replies.text:
I actually think nerd culture appealing to girls is very new, like MCU new lol 

The girls who always liked nerd stuff never needed it to “appeal” to them because they were part of the audience already

replies.author: @MoreAwsomeMetal
replies.text:
@CarloisBuriedAlive Gekk culture became popular (even towards the jocks) by the early 2000's when movies such as Harry Potter or the Lord of the Rings popularized fantasy upon the masses, not to mention the rise of the video game market who had a pretty big amount of fantasy games (most ordinary people were more like playing car games, fighting games, shooters or sports games in their prime teenage years, only nerds were pumped on RPGs and point & click games in the 90's). The democratisation of fantasy upon the masses is the point when mmore and more girls started to have another look on all the geek culture, starting to see them practice the same hobbies as the male did, but it was a younger generation than mine when this happened (It started when I was in my mid twenties, and those girls where still in high school when the shift happened). Girls of my age or older never really got into this (with very few rare exceptions) and that's why seeing someone like Max playing D&D in this show is nonsense: a girl quite self independant as her, pumped on action and skateboarding (which was rare for a girl in the 80's), and on arcade scoring would never have been into the D&D stuff. 
Even her character is anachronic: she's more like the skater kids you could see in the late 90's during the Tony Hawk vibe. There were no X-treme sports girls back in the 80's.
And when I see those kids at the end of the show that rushes in the basement to play some D&D with  some material they don't even master, it also makes me laugh: you can't play D&D (or any role playing game) if you weren't nerd enough to spend hours reading the rules before, and none of these kids depicted in the show seems to have the nerves to do this. Not to mention Holly whose character is the exact opposite of the geek: she would never spend her time on some D&D stuff in real life, especially if she was close to reach Junior High school.
The only credible character was Lucas's sister, when she was taking on her brother in the early seasons for being a complete nerd that would never get a girlfriend.
But of course, the Duffer Brothers decided that this show had to be more inclusive, and get all the chicks in the 4 boy's adventure, and strip them off their universe...
Not only was it a bad move that completely marginalized the core characters of the show, and broke that kind of innocence lying around 4 boys living their own personal fantasy in real life, but it's was also completely anachronics: The last thing you could think about  the 80's and 90's was a geek girl. It never existed, and the girls back then would have rather die than consider a single second doing any kind of geek activity of any sort...
Never mind, the show is ruined anyway since season 3, and by the end of the show, almost the whole town of Hawkins was part of that band of 4 D&D kids...

Genuine girl nerds didn’t need or want that shit because the original content was already for them too

replies.author: @coliander4180
replies.text: It wasn't the games that repulsed them. It was the people playing the games.

replies.author: @MoreAwsomeMetal
replies.text:
@coliander4180 Some nerds were pretty repulsive back in the days (many still are today), but it wasn't the only reason. I was good looking during my High school and college years, but I learnt that if you are too socially akward (such has wearing your death metal T-shirts, your cut with all the patches, playing nerd games such as board games, wargames, role palying games, collectible card games, debating with the dude on wich was the best video games, or on books or novels that weren't famous back in the days, such as Dune, Lord of the Rings, Foundation, the Elric saga, etc...) it's an instant love killer for the feminine counterpart.
Once I understood that, and learnt to keep away all those teenage habits away when I was in public (School, College, Hanging out in town, etc...), I had way more success with girls.
That's because back in the days, all the hobbies I loves were socially too far streched away from the habits of the "normal" people, and most girls are actually the keepers of the social norms.
Things changed after the coming of the Lord of the Ring, and Harry Potter on the screens, because suddenly, geek fantasy culture was made available to everybody, and became cool to the people that never read such books before.
That's when the girls, especially the younger generations that grew up with those movies started entering those nerd hobbies once they reached the teenage years: those hobbies aren't nerd exclusive, and the geek term which used to be very insulting back in the days (I still have a hard time accepting someone calling me this way) is now seen as beeing part of a positive community.

replies.author: @MoreAwsomeMetal

author: @BiologyBabe
text:
I genuinely want to know why they put the coming out scene in there. How is his being gay even relevant to the story?? Was he supposed to fight Vecna with his magical stretching butthole?? Why does it matter to the story? Can he create spit bullets with semen? WHY DOES IT MATTER??? These people think that being gay is a whole personality- but all the gay people I know don’t behave like that. They have whole parts of their lives and go weeks without their sexuality affecting anything in their life. What did will being gay have to do with the story??

Look, I have no problem with coming out scenes if they are germane to the plot and it’s something we need to know - but this reminds me of when a new coworker came up to me the first time I saw her and said “Hi, I’m (name), and I’m trans.” I was like, do you want a cookie? Are you telling e because you’re wondering if your vagina is normal? Why would I need to know what a coworker has in their pants? What could I possibly be doing at WORK that would require me to have that information? And no I don’t work in a hospital or where sex would make any difference whatsoever.

People are behaving like a whole person can just be Trans or gay, but all the people I know who really are those things don’t make it an advertisement, they just live like that because it’s what they want in their private lives, but it is irrelevant to the general public. Let’s go back to that.

Some people their whole personality is “I’m black” or “I’m gay” or “I’m trans” when things should be, “I’m everything that makes me, me.” They are pigeonholing people to think that that one thing is everything. It’s why people are getting killed for not accepting them.
